narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and plans to attend full-time, you've put real effort into your law school application, and we appreciate the dedication it took to get here. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicts an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark we use to evaluate future success in our program. This high predicted risk led to the rejection of your application, as we aim to admit students most likely to thrive and pass the bar right away. The model's decision was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, especially your Year 3 grades placing you in the 2nd decile—well below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile rank—which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the 2nd decile compared to the average around the 5th or 6th, were the next biggest factor pulling in the same concerning direction. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, lower than the usual 3.2, added to this risk signal. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out positively, as it's right around the average of 36.7 and helped nudge the prediction toward passing. Your middle family income bracket had a minor negative influence, while your full-time enrollment plans offered a small boost since most successful students follow that path. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having competitive test scores but lagging in consistent grade deciles, which the model sees as the strongest predictors of bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome early hurdles—but it explains the tough call here. To move forward, consider retaking courses or undergrad classes to boost your GPA and decile rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad work, then reapply with stronger academic proof. We're rooting for you to build that record and try again; law school could still be a great fit with these improvements. Keep pushing—you've got this.
